title: What Is Politics, Really?
objective: Understand politics as the process by which communities allocate power, resources, and values — and why this matters to everyone, not just politicians.
beginnerExplanation: Politics is simply how groups of people make decisions together. Whenever a community needs to decide who gets what — money, services, rights, protections — that is politics. It happens in parliaments, but also in local councils, workplaces, and families. The formal political system is the way a whole society makes these decisions through elections, laws, and institutions. In Australia, this means a parliamentary democracy where citizens vote for representatives who make laws on their behalf. Politics is not inherently dirty or corrupt — it is the mechanism through which a free society governs itself.
deeperExplanation: Politics, as Harold Lasswell defined it, is about 'who gets what, when, and how.' At its core, politics is the process of collective decision-making in conditions of scarcity and disagreement. Every society must decide how to distribute resources (taxation and spending), define rights (who can do what), and resolve conflicts (courts, mediation, negotiation). The Australian political system is a constitutional monarchy with a Westminster parliamentary system, three levels of government (Commonwealth, State/Territory, Local), and compulsory voting. Understanding politics means understanding power — who has it, how they got it, how they use it, and how it can be challenged. The Overton Window helps explain which ideas are considered acceptable at any given time and how political movements shift what is possible.
keyTakeaways: Politics is how societies allocate power, resources, and values collectively
It is not inherently corrupt — it is the mechanism of democratic self-governance
Australia uses a Westminster parliamentary system with three levels of government
Understanding politics means understanding power structures and how they can change
The Overton Window explains why some ideas are 'acceptable' and others are not — yet
example: Consider Medicare — Australia's universal healthcare system. It exists because of politics. In 1975, the Whitlam Government created Medibank (later dismantled, then re-created as Medicare by the Hawke Government in 1984). This was a political decision about values: should healthcare be a right or a market commodity? Both sides had legitimate arguments. The decision was made through democratic politics — elections, legislation, debate, compromise. Today, Medicare is so popular that no major party proposes abolishing it. The Overton Window has shifted: universal healthcare moved from radical to mainstream through sustained political action.
commonMistakes: Thinking politics is only about parliament — politics happens at every level of society
Believing all politicians are corrupt — most enter public service with genuine intent
Confusing politics with partisanship — politics includes non-partisan civic participation
Assuming your political views are 'common sense' rather than one position among many
Ignoring local politics — council decisions often affect your daily life more than federal ones
exercise: Map the political decisions that affect your daily life. Start from when you wake up (water supply, electricity regulation, building codes for your home) through your commute (roads, public transport subsidies), work (workplace laws, minimum wage), and evening (food safety standards, entertainment regulations). List at least 15 political decisions and identify which level of government (federal, state, local) is responsible for each. This exercise demonstrates that politics is not distant — it is the infrastructure of your daily existence.

mentalModel.title: Politics is the system for deciding together under conditions of scarcity and disagreement.
mentalModel.summary: If you remember one thing, remember this: politics is not just parties and personalities. It is the mechanism through which communities distribute power, rights, resources, and penalties.
anchors: What institution makes the decision stick?

steps: A community faces a conflict, a scarcity problem, or a question of values.
Institutions decide who is allowed to make the call.
Political actors compete to shape the outcome through persuasion, procedure, coalition, or law.
The decision gets implemented through policy, budgets, regulation, or legislation.
Citizens, courts, media, and later elections test whether the decision remains legitimate.

upsides: Politics allows conflict to be managed without constant violence.
It gives societies a lawful way to revise priorities over time.
It turns values into durable institutions and services.
downsides: Politics can become cynical performance if institutions are weak or voters are disengaged.
Short-term incentives can distort long-term public good.
The loudest or best organised actors can overpower quieter interests.
teaching.workedExample: Medicare is a strong Australian example because it shows politics as a system of values plus institutions. A government chose a model of healthcare, legislation and administration made it real, opponents contested it, and over time the public turned it into mainstream common sense.
realWorldApplications: Understanding why some public services survive changes of government while others do not
Seeing council, state, and federal decisions as part of one political ecosystem
Recognising how legitimacy matters as much as technical competence

goDeeper.label: Open the politics system map
goDeeper.description: See the field laid out as regimes, institutions, processes, and careers.

goDeeper.label: Continue into regimes and ideologies
goDeeper.description: Learn how different systems justify and organise power.

quiz.question: According to Harold Lasswell, what is politics fundamentally about?
options: Who gets what, when, and how
The struggle between left and right
quiz.explanation: Lasswell's classic definition frames politics as the process of allocating scarce resources and values — who gets what, when, and how. This goes beyond partisanship to the fundamental mechanism of collective decision-making.

quiz.question: How many levels of government does Australia have?
options: Four: Federal, State, Territory, and Local
quiz.explanation: Australia has three levels of government: the Commonwealth (federal), State and Territory governments, and Local government (councils). Each has different responsibilities defined by the Constitution and various acts.

quiz.question: What does the Overton Window describe?
options: The physical windows of Parliament House
The range of policies politically acceptable to the mainstream at a given time
quiz.explanation: The Overton Window describes the range of ideas and policies that are considered politically acceptable or mainstream at any given moment. Political movements work to shift this window to make previously radical ideas acceptable.

perspectives.citizen: Politics determines the quality of your schools, hospitals, roads, and rights. Understanding it is not optional — it is the price of living in a democracy.
perspectives.staffer: Your job is to translate the messy reality of competing interests into coherent policy positions your member can defend publicly.
perspectives.politician: Politics is your medium. Just as an artist works in paint, you work in persuasion, compromise, and collective decision-making.
perspectives.strategist: Every political environment has an Overton Window. Your job is to know where it sits and decide whether to work within it or move it.
